value:
The number of construction companies in ‘critical’ financial distress rose by nearly 50 per cent year-on-year in the first quarter of 2026, according to an insolvency specialist.
BTG, formerly known as Begbies Traynor, said there were 9,466 UK construction businesses in the category during the first three months of 2026.
This was up 49 per cent on the 6,367 recorded in the first quarter of 2025.
The number in the category did fall by 5 per cent compared with the last quarter of 2025, BTG added, but it said this was part of a seasonal trend rather than a sector improvement.
Ratings are given as part of BTG’s Red Flag Alert system, which screens companies for sustained or marked deterioration in key financial ratios and indicators.
Metrics include working capital, contingent liabilities, retained profits and net worth.
Critical distress rose most sharply among those whose business is categorised at Companies House as ‘construction of domestic buildings’ and ‘electrical installation’, which jumped 55 per cent and 51 per cent year-on-year respectively.
The number of construction firms in ‘significant’ distress, the next category down, rose to 95,355, making it the sector with the highest numbers of companies in distress.
BTG managing partner Julie Palmer said: “Construction has always been a barometer for the UK economy and with thousands of businesses in distress this could ripple wider if a turnaround doesn’t happen soon.
“Unfortunately, even though some large developers are highlighting their ability to meet targets, the tide of challenges keeps rising and this is making a turnaround even more difficult.”
She added that fuel prices, material inflation, supply chain disruption and interest rates were adding to existing issues like planning bottlenecks and a skills shortage.
“This is creating a real risk that construction firms and developers will have to revisit the drawing board with accounts departments and lenders to negotiate a way forward. The financial playing field is shifting again.
“In the coming months there needs to be careful assessment of what and who is needed to survive in these conditions. Infrastructure and homes need to be built, but if the builder is unable to make the cost stack up or the end buyer is put off by the cost of a mortgage, or is uncertain about their job prospects, then the appeal starts to wane.”
Data from the Insolvency Service earlier this month revealed that construction insolvencies grew to 301 in February 2026, although were down from 367 in the same month in 2025.
Earlier this month, Creditsafe data exclusively revealed by Construction News showed there were 36 construction sector administrations in March, up from 22 in February and the highest March total since 2023.
